title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Two Wheels and Two Centuries: The Story of the Bicycle
content.text: Few inventions have changed human life as quietly and as completely as the bicycle. It requires no fuel, produces no emissions and grants its rider a freedom of movement that was, for most of human history, available only to those wealthy enough to own a horse. (37) [37] Su impacto social fue especialmente importante para ciertos grupos. [/37] For women in the nineteenth century, in particular, it represented a genuine social revolution, enabling independent travel at a time when respectable women were not expected to venture far from home. || The earliest bicycle-like machine appeared in Germany in 1817. Known as the draisine or 'running machine', it had no pedals; the rider propelled it by pushing against the ground with their feet. (38) [38] Durante décadas, el diseño evolucionó de forma irregular. [/38] The pedalled velocipede, nicknamed the 'boneshaker' for obvious reasons, arrived in the 1860s, followed by the high-wheeled penny-farthing of the 1870s — an elegant but unstable design that required considerable courage to ride. || The safety bicycle, introduced in the 1880s, transformed the industry. With two wheels of equal size, a chain drive and, later, pneumatic tyres, it was finally practical for ordinary people. (39) [39] La bicicleta de seguridad democratizó el transporte personal. [/39] Production soared, prices fell, and cycling clubs sprang up across Europe and North America. || The bicycle's fortunes dipped in the twentieth century as the automobile took hold. (40) [40] El automóvil desplazó a la bicicleta durante décadas. [/40] Cycling retreated to the margins in many wealthy countries, seen as the transport of those who could not afford a car. Yet in the Netherlands and Denmark, cycling infrastructure continued to develop, laying the groundwork for the models that urban planners now study and copy worldwide. || In recent decades, the bicycle has staged a remarkable comeback. Concern about climate change, urban congestion and personal health has persuaded millions to return to two wheels. (41) [41] Las ciudades han respondido invirtiendo en infraestructura ciclista. [/41] Electric-assist bikes have further widened the appeal, making cycling accessible to older riders and those facing hilly terrain. || Today, the bicycle remains the most efficient form of human-powered transport ever devised. (42) [42] Su sencillez es también su mayor fortaleza. [/42] It converts human energy into forward motion more efficiently than walking, running or any comparable activity — a fact that engineers, after two centuries of trying, have yet to improve upon.

paragraphs.A: Several competing designs appeared in rapid succession, each attempting to solve the same basic problems of balance and propulsion.
paragraphs.B: Its basic mechanics — a frame, two wheels, pedals and a chain — have changed surprisingly little in a hundred and thirty years.
paragraphs.C: Cities began investing in dedicated cycle lanes and bike-sharing schemes as part of broader sustainability strategies.
paragraphs.D: For several decades, car ownership became the dominant aspiration, and the bicycle was largely forgotten.
paragraphs.E: Sales of private cars surged after the Second World War, and road building swallowed the budgets that might have gone to cycle paths.
paragraphs.F: Mass production meant that a working-class family could own a bicycle for the first time.
paragraphs.G: Its social impact was felt most keenly by those who had previously been denied independent mobility.

questions.explanation: G introduces the social impact of the bicycle before the specific example of women in the nineteenth century.
A summarises the uneven evolution of design before the specific models (velocipede, penny-farthing) named below.
F explains how mass production made the bicycle cheaper, which leads directly to the rise of cycling clubs.
D introduces the decline of the bicycle against the car before mentioning the countries that maintained cycling infrastructure.
C describes the urban response with cycle lanes and lending schemes, continuing the theme of the bicycle's return.
B highlights the almost unchanged simplicity of the design, supporting the final statement about the bicycle's unbeatable efficiency.
